subject: ap-chemistry
title: Chemical Kinetics & Rate Laws
stimulus:
The following equation represents the decomposition of $N_2O_5$, for which the rate law is rate = k[$N_2O_5$].

2 $N_2O_5$(g) → 4 $NO_2$(g) + $O_2$(g)

A sample of pure $N_2O_5$(g) is placed in an evacuated container and allowed to decompose at a constant temperature of 300 K. The concentration of $N_2O_5$(g) in the container is measured over a period of time, and the measurements are recorded in the following table.

| Time (hr) | [$N_2O_5$] (M) |
|-----------|----------------|
| 0 | 0.160 |
| 1.67 | 0.0800 |
| 3.33 | 0.0400 |
| 5.00 | 0.0200 |

parts.prompt: Determine the value of the rate constant, k , for the reaction. Include units in your answer.

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated value: 1 point

required_elements: Provide a correct response from the accepted alternatives

scoring_points.description: For the correct units, consistent with the calculated value: 1 point

required_elements: For the correct units, consistent with the calculated value: 1 point

parts.prompt:
The following mechanism is proposed for the decomposition of $N_2O_5$(g).

Step 1: $N_2O_5$(g) → $NO_2$(g) + $NO_3$(g)
Step 2: $NO_2$(g) + $NO_3$(g) → $NO_2$(g) + NO(g) + $O_2$(g)
Step 3: $N_2O_5$(g) + NO(g) → 3 $NO_2$(g)

Identify which step of the proposed mechanism (1, 2, or 3) is the rate-determining step. Justify your answer in terms of the rate law given.

scoring_points.description: For the correct answer and a valid justification: 1 point

required_elements: For the correct answer and a valid justification: 1 point
alternatives.correct_example: Step 1 is the rate-determining step. The rate law of elementary step 1 is rate = k[$N_2O_5$], which matches the given rate law.

parts.prompt: If this experiment was repeated at the same temperature but with twice the initial concentration of $N_2O_5$, would the value of k increase, decrease, or remain the same? Explain your reasoning.

alternatives.correct_example: Remain the same. The rate constant, k, is independent of concentration and will remain the
